faq.question: What can I sign?
faq.answer: Sigstore tooling supports signing any artifacts, such as files, containers, binaries, and attestations. [Cosign](https://github.com/sigstore/cosign) is excellent for signing files, binaries, and containers. [Gitsign](https://github.com/sigstore/gitsign) is specialized for git commits and the [Model Transparency](https://github.com/sigstore/model-transparency) project is perfect for signing ML models.

faq.question: Is Sigstore easy to use?
faq.answer: Signing and verifying software benefits the entire ecosystem, so we have made our tools easy to adopt and use. You can see how easy it is by taking a look at our [Cosign quickstart guide](https://docs.sigstore.dev/quickstart/quickstart-cosign/) or our [CI quickstart guide](https://docs.sigstore.dev/quickstart/quickstart-ci/). The Sigstore project is also customizable and modular, with support for deploying self-hosted infrastructure. Detailed information on each Sigstore tool is available in our [developer documentation](https://docs.sigstore.dev/).

faq.question: Who can benefit from using Sigstore?
faq.answer:
Sigstore helps many types of people solve supply chain security problems.
- Open source users can verify signatures on their package dependencies. This allows for catching compromised packages and protecting a project from an insecure supply chain. Open source users also benefit from upstream Sigstore adoption by open source maintainers and language communities. 
- Open source maintainers can easily sign their releases and provide their users with a way to ensure that the project has not been compromised. Similarly to open source users, maintainers can gain confidence in the integrity of their package dependencies and pass that confidence on to their users. 
- Sigstore has been adopted by a number of package managers (for example, [PyPI](https://blog.sigstore.dev/pypi-attestations-ga/) and [npm](https://blog.sigstore.dev/cosign-verify-bundles/)) to create a build provenance for their packages. Such a provenance links users back to a package's source code and build instructions, providing users with important supply chain information and increasing the security and transparency of the registry. [OpenSSF's goal](https://github.com/ossf/wg-securing-software-repos/blob/main/docs/build-provenance-for-all-package-registries.md) is to see all package registries providing build provenance, and Sigstore is a great way to do so.  
 - Security professionals can adopt Sigstore within their organization to streamline supply chain security practice. Our tools are easy to implement, and can be built into your CI system allowing for easy adoption by developer teams.

faq.question: How do I learn more about Sigstore?
faq.answer: Our [documentation](https://docs.sigstore.dev) includes extensive information about all the tools that make up Sigstore. It also includes quickstart guides, a discussion of our security model, and information about our language-specific clients and CI integrations. Other resources include the project's [YouTube channel](https://www.youtube.com/@projectsigstore), the [OpenSSF's website](https://openssf.org/), and the [Sigstore Blog](https://blog.sigstore.dev/).

faq.question: How do I get help if I have a question?
faq.answer: The fastest way to get answers, ideas and feedback is on [Sigstore's Slack](https://sigstore.slack.com/join/shared_invite/zt-2ub0ztl5z-PkWb_Ldwef5d6nb~oryaTA), or to post an issue on one of [Sigstore's repositories](https://github.com/sigstore).

faq.question: How can I get involved?
faq.answer: The best way is to get involved is to try out the technologies and let us know your thoughts! We welcome new members to the working group over on [Slack](https://sigstore.slack.com/join/shared_invite/zt-2ub0ztl5z-PkWb_Ldwef5d6nb~oryaTA) and you can join the mailing list at sigstore-dev@googlegroups.com. You are also welcome to join us on our community calls. Meetings are held every other Thursday. The time alternates to accomodate more timezones. Details are available on the [OpenSSF Calendar](https://openssf.org/calendar/).

faq.question: What is Sigstore's current status?
faq.answer: Sigstore is an actively maintained, OpenSSF-graduated project. Sigstore is made up of a number of tools, all of which have their own release schedule and project goals. For latest project information, please see our [blog](https://blog.sigstore.dev/) or individual [project repositories](https://github.com/orgs/sigstore/repositories).
